title: Contact Non-Squeezing in Various Closed Prequantizations
speaker: Pierre-Alexandre Arlove
abstract:
I will describe and argue the existence of contact non-squeezing phenomena in contact lens spaces and in strongly orderable prequantizations.
The proof is based on the construction of contact capacities coming from spectral selectors defined on the contactomorphisms group of the latter contact manifolds. I will define all these notions during my talk.

proof_stages.label: Motivation from symplectic rigidity and contact flexibility
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls Darboux local normal forms, Gromov symplectic nonsqueezing, and the absence of local contact nonsqueezing, motivating an ambient notion of contact squeezing and situating the work relative to earlier open-prequantization results.

proof_stages.label: Closed prequantization setup and theorem statement
proof_stages.summary: Reeb flows, closed prequantizations, the quotients M_k with Reeb period 2pi/k, and the main nonsqueezing theorem are defined, with hypotheses given by the standard sphere/lens-space family or by strong orderability.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to a contact capacity
proof_stages.summary: A contact capacity on open subsets of M_k is postulated with monotonicity, rounded contactomorphism invariance, and upper/lower estimates on contact balls, and these properties are shown to imply the nonsqueezing theorem.

proof_stages.label: Translated-point spectrum and selector axioms
proof_stages.summary: The speaker passes to the universal cover of the contactomorphism group, defines discriminant and translated points and their spectrum, and states the spectral-selector theorem with triangle, order, nondegeneracy, and lift-compatibility properties.

proof_stages.label: Construction of the spectral selectors
proof_stages.summary: For sphere/lens-space prequantizations the selector is built from the nonlinear Maslov index, while for strongly orderable prequantizations it is built from a minimax positive length of Legendrian isotopies in M x M x R.

proof_stages.label: Capacity properties from spectral selectors
proof_stages.summary: The capacity is defined as the supremum of selectors over contact isotopies supported in a domain, then monotonicity, rounded conjugation invariance via periodic Reeb flow, and a displacement energy-capacity inequality are established.

proof_stages.label: Contact-ball estimates and conclusion
proof_stages.summary: The upper bound for the capacity of a contact ball is obtained from refined displacement, while the lower bound is obtained by constructing strict contactomorphisms in M_1 with controlled spectrum and descending them to M_k, completing the proof.

examples.name: Gromov ball-in-cylinder nonsqueezing
examples.note: The classical symplectic embedding problem for a ball into a cylinder is used as a benchmark showing rigidity beyond volume preservation.
examples.engagement_reason: The Gromov ball-in-cylinder theorem is stated as background, with no computation or proof worked through on the ball/cylinder example.

examples.engagement_reason: Large balls in complex projective space are invoked via a cited embedding result to note an application at k=2, without working out the embedding or radii.

examples.name: Unit cotangent bundles with periodic geodesic flow
examples.note: A closed Riemannian manifold with common-period geodesic flow gives a unit cotangent bundle that is strongly orderable and hence falls under the theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: Unit cotangent bundles with periodic geodesic flow are given as a class of examples where the theorem applies, with properties cited rather than derived.

examples.name: Aspherical integral symplectic prequantizations
examples.note: An integral symplectic manifold with aspherical symplectic form yields a hyper-tight, strongly orderable prequantization, illustrating another source of hypotheses for the theorem.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker outlines the prequantization construction from an integral symplectic form and explains why the aspherical case becomes hypertight and hence strongly orderable.

examples.name: Naive translation displacing a contact ball
examples.note: A simple translation of the contact ball gives only a 4r^2 energy bound, showing why a more careful displacement is needed to obtain the sharp pi r^2 upper estimate.
examples.engagement_reason: The naive translation of a contact ball is explicitly used as a displacement attempt and its energy estimate is discussed, including why the resulting bound is not sharp enough.
